signals.title: AUD/USD — Geopolitical Shock + RBA Policy + Retail Divergence
signals.text: AUD/USD at 0.7169 facing acute volatility as WTI crude surges +4.39% on Iran tensions. While RBA (4.10%) vs Fed (3.75%) provides a +35bp carry advantage, the inflationary shock from energy prices reinforces hawkish expectations for today's RBA meeting. COT LF net long +48,299 (74.8% long) shows institutional backing, while retail sentiment is 80% short—a significant divergence. Institutional positioning and carry favor the upside, but geopolitical risk-off flows to the USD (DXY 98.48) create a friction point. Implication: Retail capitulation risk is high if RBA delivers a hawkish surprise amid energy-driven inflation.

signals.title: NZD/USD — COT vs OIS Divergence + RBNZ
signals.text: NZD/USD COT LF net short -16,833 (long% 23.0%) shows speculators heavily fading the kiwi despite a hawkish RBNZ market bias. With RBNZ at 2.25% and the market pricing a hike for the May 27 meeting, a clear divergence exists between bearish spec positioning and hawkish central bank pricing. NZD/USD at 0.5873 remains under pressure from the USD carry advantage (+150bp), but the crowded spec short creates elevated short-squeeze risk if RBNZ rhetoric remains aggressive. Implication: Speculative unwind targets 0.6000 if RBNZ confirms hike path.

signals.title: EUR/USD — Correlation Break + Energy Shock
signals.text: EUR/USD vs EuroStoxx correlation at +0.709 vs 252d norm of -0.154 (z=+2.01σ) signals a regime shift where EUR is trading as a pure proxy for European risk assets. ECB forecasters raising inflation outlook to 2.7% while slashing growth due to energy shocks weighs on the pair. EUR/USD at 1.1693 is holding below the 1.1700 handle as the Fed-ECB +175bp carry spread favors the USD. Implication: EUR/USD upside is structurally limited by the growth-inflation trade-off; the pair remains a sell-on-rallies candidate while the energy shock persists.

signals.title: USD/JPY — Carry vs Intervention Risk
signals.text: USD/JPY at 157.24 supported by a +300bp Fed-BoJ carry premium. JPY COT LF net short -88,543 (30.6% long) remains crowded, reflecting the dominant carry trade. Despite Middle East tensions, VIX at 18.3 (normal volatility) suggests safe-haven JPY demand is not yet displacing carry flows. However, the MOVE index rising +10.58% to 77.9 indicates increasing bond market volatility which typically precedes JPY volatility. Implication: Carry remains the primary driver until VIX > 25, but 160.00 remains the critical watch level for BoJ verbal intervention.
